source.title: Why behavior change is difficult to sustain

confidence_rationale: Net supports modelling deferred/blocked goals as persistent context-recoverable OPERANT traces (extends Bouton 2004 from Pavlovian cues to voluntary action policies -- directly the bank's case). But it specifically and strongly WEAKENS the recoverability-gate-can-close assumption: accumulated suppression evidence (36 vs 4 sessions, identical resurgence) does not retire the trace, so a literal recoverability cutoff is biologically unsupported. Confidence moderate: behavioural-only, clinically framed, no quantitative gate.
failure_signatures: Resurgence: a deferred goal that was successfully replaced by an alternative returns the moment the alternative stops paying off -- relapse triggered by loss of the substitute, not by the original cue (retiring an anchor because a competitor is winning is unsafe).

Extended elimination training does not reduce return (36 sessions = 4 sessions resurgence): more evidence of hopelessness does not make the trace safer to drop; an evidence-integrating recoverability gate will be falsely confident.
Rapid reacquisition: one lapse restores the full behaviour because the reinforced trial is itself part of the original context.
Reinstatement after punishment: aversively suppressed behaviour returns after a single outcome reminder -- knowledge of bad consequences does not durably retire the trace.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Operant behaviour change (extinction, counterconditioning, punishment, alternative reinforcement, abstinence) inhibits but never erases the original response, and that inhibition is highly context-specific; operant renewal, spontaneous recovery, reinstatement, rapid reacquisition and resurgence are all demonstrated, including in drug self-administration. Longer/stronger elimination training does not monotonically reduce return.
mapping.ree_translation: Extends the preserve-don't-erase result from Pavlovian cues to voluntary action policies -- which is what ghost-bank entries actually are -- so ARC-060/MECH-292 inherit the operant evidence directly. Crucially it constrains MECH-292's recoverability term: the gate cannot be a function that integrates accumulated suppression evidence toward 'retire this anchor', because biology shows no dose of such evidence closes the gate; recoverability must be risk-reduction, not erasure, and 'competitor is winning' is not a safe retire condition (resurgence).
mapping.mapping_caveat: Behavioural mechanism only; 'context' is loosely operationalised (place, time, recent reinforcers, mood, recent behaviours). The load-bearing transfer risk: there is no empirically supported 'safe to retire' state, which directly contradicts any recoverability cutoff that accumulates suppression evidence -- an artificial system may still need an explicit abandon path, but it cannot be justified by 'enough evidence of hopelessness'.
mapping.source_context: Clinically framed review of operant extinction/relapse phenomena, including drug self-administration and health-behaviour-change relapse (~70% one-year relapse cited).

confidence_components.notes: Source quality good (recent, clinically explicit, operant + drug self-admin breadth). Mapping fidelity moderate-high: operant traces ARE the bank's case. Transfer risk moderate: behavioural-only; the strong claim is a falsifier of a specific gate design, which is itself a high-value mapping.
